Dublin Hurling and Football Contests

e's surprising that so very few spectators
attended at Croke Park on Sunday last.
Four first-class hurling and football teams
made their first appearance for the present
season, and a few odd hundreds were only
interested enough to come to see chem.

However, those who were absent missed
atreat. Two rattling contests were wit
nessed, either of which would have proved
value for the entrance fee. Rapparees v.
Commercials, trying conclusions in hurling,
was in many respects a more exhilarating
affair than the All-Ireland hurling final
played on the same historic sod a few weeks
since, It was a neck-and-neck race to the
finish, and if there was not an even greater
thriller to follow, in the shape of a football
contest between ‘Geraldines' and Keatings,
the odd hundreds present would have gone
home well pleased.

‘Vigorous Hurling

‘The hurling was vigorous—at times a

tuifle over-vigorous, with the result that
three players finished up on the side-line a5
spectators. In the opening moiety the ex.
changes were very fast, and the scoring
alternating. Maher and Kennedy were
the principal scorers’ for Raps, while
Dunne and Leonard were iainly instru
‘mental in putting the Commercials “ahead
at the interval by 4-2 to 4-0.

After a lively resumption, Commercials
settled down co the attack, bur the defence
of Kennedy and Hackest was’ good, and

~ Mahony put Rapparees abead with a goal,
but Legnatd restored the balance with a
point, after some graud ground-play. The

of the remainiog play was briefly -

Commercials did by tar the most of
king, but they were singularly un-
iatter of wives, while Coon,

coming ned we The Commercials?
defence was slow and not always. depen
fable, with the result that some surprise
visits by Maher and Kennedy were: pro-
fictive of majors. Comnineccials were un-
on the aay score—Rapparees,
